topic: A student learns their scholarship application was denied
raw_output:
[story 1]

The rejection email sat glowing on the screen, a stark white rectangle mocking the effort poured into those essays. Liam reread the line, "We regret to inform you that your application was not selected for the scholarship," and his hands began to tremble. He stared at the ceiling, tracing the cracks in the plaster with his eyes, the silence of his small room amplifying the frantic thumping in his chest. He felt a sudden, hot flush creep up his neck, making him want to physically shrink into the worn fabric of his hoodie. Every perfectly polished sentence he’d crafted, every late night spent wrestling with complex ideas, felt suddenly hollow, invalidated by this single, impersonal notification. He quickly minimized the email, shoving the laptop lid shut with a decisive, almost violent snap, desperate to escape the visible evidence of his failure.

[story 2]

Maya kept glancing at the framed photo on her desk—a picture of her smiling proudly at a graduation ceremony—and felt a tightness constrict around her throat. She picked up her pen, but the plastic felt slick and foreign in her grip. The rejection letter lay beside her notebook, its formal typeface seeming to judge her every move. She started pacing the narrow confines of her dorm room, her footsteps echoing too loudly on the linoleum floor. When she stopped by the window, she looked out at the bustling campus, the vibrant energy of successful students seeming impossibly distant. A dry, bitter taste coated her tongue. She forced herself to sit down, pulling her knees to her chest, trying to make her posture smaller, less noticeable, wishing the floor would swallow her whole.

[story 3]

"No." The single word echoed in the quiet apartment. Alex stared at the screen, his breath catching in his throat. He reread the notification three times, each repetition scraping against a raw nerve. His jaw tightened until the muscles ached. He couldn't look away from the words confirming his exclusion; they were too final, too absolute. He picked up his phone, intending to call a friend, but his fingers hovered over the dial pad, paralyzed by the need to face the reality alone. He slowly lowered the phone, letting it clatter onto the bedside table. He stood up abruptly, walking to the mirror, his reflection staring back—a pale, uncomposed figure whose shoulders seemed too large for the space they occupied. He quickly smoothed down his shirt, a nervous habit born of wanting to appear put-together when everything inside felt completely unraveling.
